vulnerability.title: Enable Kernel Parameter to Use Reverse Path Filtering on all IPv4 Interfaces by Default
vulnerability.description: To set the runtime status of the net.ipv4.conf.default.rp_filter kernel parameter, run the following command: $ sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.conf.default.rp_filter=1 To make sure that the setting is persistent, add the following line to a file in the directory /etc/sysctl.d: net.ipv4.conf.default.rp_filter = 1
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then # Comment out any occurrences of net.ipv4.conf.default.rp_filter from /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf files for f in /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf /run/sysctl.d/*.conf /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf; do # skip systemd-sysctl symlink (/etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf -> /etc/sysctl.conf) if [[ "$(readlink -f "$f")" == "/etc/sysctl.conf" ]]; then continue; fi matching_list=$(grep -P '^(?!#).*[\s]*net.ipv4.conf.default.rp_filter.*$' $f | uniq ) if ! test -z "$matching_list"; then while IFS= read -r entry; do escaped_entry=$(sed -e 's|/|\\/|g' <<< "$entry") # comment out "net.ipv4.conf.default.rp_filter" matches to preserve user data sed -i --follow-symlinks "s/^${escaped_entry}$/# &/g" $f done <<< "$matching_list" fi done # # S

triage_decision.reason: Setting net.ipv4.conf.default.rp_filter to 1 is a standard kernel parameter hardening measure that does not affect authentication, authorization, or critical services. It follows STIG guidelines and is unlikely to cause lockout or system breakage.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: networking affected; Potential impact on non-standard routing configurations

reasoning_messages.reasoning:
We need to do recommended change: set net.ipv4.conf.default.rp_filter=1 in /etc/sysctl.d with persistent config. Also maybe we need to comment out any existing occurrences. According to rule: we can just add a file /etc/sysctl.d/99-ssg.conf or something. Let's propose plan: Check if file /etc/sysctl.d/99-ssg.conf exists; if not, create. Use read_file to check content: if any net.ipv4.conf.default.rp_filter line exists in any sysctl.d file, comment out. Simpler: Append new line to a new file ensuring no duplicates. To avoid duplication we check if the line exists.

Plan: Inspect /etc/sysctl.d/99-ssg.conf (create if missing). If already has net.ipv4.conf.default.rp_filter=1, done. Else, if any line containing the parameter exists, comment it out. Then add the line at end.

Need to use sed -i. Use read_file to inspect current sysctl files patterns: we can check all /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf for the line not commented. We'll just read and then use sed. But we need to use a single command per run. So let's do read_file commands to read each file, modify via sed. But easier: we can just use run_cmd with sed to comment out existing lines in all config files.

Sequence:

1. Use find to list relevant files: /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf /run/sysctl.d/*.conf /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf.
Because we cannot chain commands, for find we may need to run one command that edits multiple files? The spec says no && or semicolons. We could chain multiple run_cmd calls for each file found. That might be cumbersome. Perhaps simpler: just add a new file and ignore duplicate: scanning might duplicate. But risk of duplicate? Since rule says do not duplicate. We can use sed -i to comment out occurrences in each file. But we cannot loop. We could write a shell command like `sed -i '/^net.ipv4.conf.default.rp_filter/ s/^/#/' /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf /run/sysctl.d/*.conf /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf` but that uses globbing; no chaining. It's single command. That is allowed: we are using sed to modify multiple file names. That's okay; we just run a single command. But should not pipe? Using sed. It's allowed. We'll run `sed -i 's/^\(net\.ipv4\.conf\.default\.rp_filter\)/#\1/' /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf /run/sysctl.d/*.conf /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf` But to comment only the line that starts with that key and not a commented line. The regex pattern: `^\s*net\.ipv4\.conf\.default\.rp_filter` then replace start with #. Also need to avoid lines that already have comment. This sed will comment all those lines regardless of commented status. But if lines already commented with '#', then it will comment them again leading to '# #', but it's okay? Might produce double comment. We can restrict to lines not starting with '#': `s/^\s*\(net\.ipv4\.conf\.default\.rp_filter\)/#\1/` but that might comment only those lines as they start without whitespace? Might have whitespace. Use `s/^\(\s*\)net\.ipv4\.conf\.default\.rp_filter/#\1net.ipv4.conf.default.rp_filter/`. But easier to just run `sed -i 's/^\s*net\.ipv4\.conf\.default\.rp_filter/#&/' ...` This will comment lines. It will also comment the comment lines producing '# #line' but only those lines that start with any whitespace then the parameter, ignoring lines that start with '#'. But if the line already starts with '#', the regex won't match because it starts with '#'. Good.

Thus plan: sed -i to comment existing lines. Then create file /etc/sysctl.d/99-ssg.conf and write the line with `net.ipv4.conf.default.rp_filter = 1`.

Need to read current file before modifying? Not strictly needed, but rule says always use read_file to inspect before modifying. So step: read_file /etc/sysctl.d/99-ssg.conf to see if exists. Because maybe we can just write.

Pretend not exist, but we can try to read and if fails, proceed.

Thus steps:

1. read_file /etc/sysctl.d/99-ssg.conf – if success, check content. If it already contains the line, skip. If contains another line for rp_filter, comment that via sed.

But we can't rely on exit code logic inside `read_file`. But we can get output.

We'll assume reader: read_file returns content, maybe error. The behavior? We'll assume it's okay.

So plan: read_file /etc/sysctl.d/99-ssg.conf. Then we check output; if contains "net.ipv4.conf.default.rp_filter" then we will comment via sed? But simpler: always create file and ensure line present; if file already contains line, `write_file` with same content will overwrite. Could modify or deduplicate.

Step: We'll just create file with the line; if file already present it's fine.

But rule: Do NOT duplicate config lines. So better to read file and check if line exists; if yes, do nothing. If no, or file missing, write file.

Thus approach: plan:

- Use read_file to read /etc/sysctl.d/99-ssg.conf. If returns content containing net.ipv4.conf.default.rp_filter, skip writing.

- Then use sed -i to comment any existing lines across sysctl.d directories.

- Then write /etc/sysctl.d/99-ssg.conf with line.

Simplify: use sed for comment in all files, skip if no file exists? If file doesn't exist, sed will error; we can use a command that silently fails if file not found? Not guaranteed. We'll need to handle each glob? We could use `sed -i ... /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf /run/sysctl.d/*.conf /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf` but for nonexistent files, it will error. Might be okay.

Better to check existence: Use `run_cmd` with `test -f /etc/sysctl.d/99-ssg.conf` to see. But that isn't read_file. But rule didn't forbid run_cmd to check. But "Always use read_file to inspect target config file BEFORE modifying it." That applies to config file modifications. But we might still call run_cmd for existence check? But to be safe, we can just read file; if fails, treat as empty.

Implementation plan:

- read_file /etc/sysctl.d/99-ssg.conf. if output contains 'net.ipv4.conf.default.rp_filter', set flag that it exists.

- If not exists, run sed to comment existing lines across directories. `sed -i 's/^\(\s*\)net\.ipv4\.conf\.default\.rp_filter/#\1net.ipv4.conf.default.rp_filter/' /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf /run/sysctl.d/*.conf /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf` (This will comment any line in those files). To prevent error if no files, we could use `find` with `-type f -name "*.conf" -exec sed -i ... {} +`, but that's multiple commands. Let's keep simple: maybe okay if no file.

- Then write_file /etc/sysctl.d/99-ssg.conf content "net.ipv4.conf.default.rp_filter = 1\n".

Note: Use mode 644.

Ok. Now produce plan.
